items.question: What is the difference between a class and an object?
items.answer: A class is the blueprint that defines attributes and methods; an object (instance) is a concrete value created from that blueprint with its own attribute data. One class can produce many independent objects.

items.question: What is the difference between instance attributes and class attributes?
items.answer: Instance attributes are set per object (usually via self in __init__) and are independent across instances. Class attributes are defined in the class body and shared by all instances. Sharing a mutable class attribute is a common bug because edits leak across instances.

items.question: When would you use @classmethod versus @staticmethod?
items.answer: @classmethod receives the class (cls) and is often used for alternative constructors (e.g. Client.from_env()). @staticmethod receives neither self nor cls and is just a function grouped with the class for organization. A plain instance method receives self.

items.question: What does super() do?
items.answer: super() returns a proxy to the parent (next in the method resolution order) so you can call the parent's implementation — most commonly super().__init__(...) to run the parent's setup before adding subclass-specific state.

items.question: What is duck typing and why does Python rely on it?
items.answer: Duck typing means an object's suitability is determined by the methods/attributes it has, not its class. If an object has a run() method, it can be used wherever a 'tool' is expected. This enables flexible, loosely-coupled code without rigid type hierarchies.
